subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands how different parts of the brain have special jobs and what happens if those parts get hurt.
Your child can describe the jobs of helpers and leaders like principals, custodians, volunteers, police, and firefighters.
Your child understands that kidneys remove waste from the blood and turn it into urine, which is stored in the bladder.
Your child knows how to ask for help when they need it.
Your child can describe when there is an emergency and who to ask for help.
Your child can identify healthy foods and activities that help keep the body strong.
Your child can look at information from different websites to find medical facts that might not be true.
Your child can find health information from home, school, and community sources.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Human Body — Health, Safety, and Community Helpers
coherence_reason: The standards address human body functions, health habits, safety, and roles of helpers, which are related but represent distinct sub-topics within health and safety.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can ask questions to learn why weather forecasts help people get ready for bad weather.
Your child can use weather maps and data to predict local weather and knows forecasts depend on many factors.
Your child knows what causes different severe weather and what effects they have.
Your child understands how the ocean affects weather and how the water cycle influences weather patterns.
Natural Hazards
Your child asks questions to learn why weather forecasts help people get ready for bad weather.
Your child asks questions to learn why weather forecasts help us get ready for bad weather.
Your child can tell what happens before a storm, like the sky getting dark, the temperature dropping, and the wind getting stronger.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Weather — Forecasts, Natural Hazards, and Preparation
coherence_reason: All standards relate to understanding weather, how to predict it, and how to prepare for severe weather, with some minor variation in emphasis (e.g., water cycle and oceans).

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can observe and describe shapes by their important features, sort and draw them, and begin learning about fractions by dividing shapes.
Your child can locate all the continents on a map or globe.
Your child can find examples of goods (things you can buy) and services (help people provide).
Your child can tell the difference between land shapes and bodies of water on maps and globes.
Your child can compare how different countries and regions trade with each other.
Your child can compare how people, families, and communities are alike and different in their cultures.
Your child can tell the difference between goods (things you buy) and services (help people provide).
Your child can name different body parts animals use to see, hear, move, and grab things.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Life Science — Animal Body Parts and Their Functions
coherence_reason: The standards cluster covers multiple unrelated topics: geometry, geography, economics, culture, and animal body parts.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can find important lines on maps and globes like the prime meridian, equator, Tropics of Capricorn and Cancer, International Date Line, and lines of latitude and longitude.
Your child knows the solar system includes Earth, the Moon, the Sun, eight other planets with their moons, and smaller objects like asteroids and comets.
Your child can connect the Sun with daytime.
Your child can identify the Moon in the night sky.
Your child knows the Sun is seen during the day.
Your child can notice big and small things in the sky.
Your child understands that day and night happen again and again in a pattern.
Your child can find the Sun in the sky during the day.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Patterns in the Sky — Sun, Moon, and Day/Night
coherence_reason: Most standards address recognizing the Sun, Moon, and day/night cycle in the sky, but one standard is about map lines and another about the broad solar system, which are distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Observing Sun, Moon, and day/night patterns
Identifying map lines and solar system objects

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will observe and describe how sunlight affects the Earth's surface.
Your child knows the Sun is the biggest object in the solar system and is mostly made of hydrogen and helium.
Your child knows that a planet moves around the Sun because of the Sun’s gravity pulling on it.
Your child understands that when water evaporates, it becomes vapor in the air and can turn back into liquid or ice when cooled.
Your child knows uneven heating of Earth causes air to move in currents.
Earth's Systems
Definitions of Energy
Earth and the Solar System
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Earth and Space — Sun's Effects and Solar System Basics
coherence_reason: The standards group both the Sun's effect on Earth's surface and basic facts about the solar system, as well as weather and energy concepts, which are related but represent at least two distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Sun and Earth's surface (energy, sunlight, heating, evaporation)

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can save, find, change, and delete information on a computer and knows this information is called data.
Your child can name and describe what common computer parts do, like the mouse, keyboard, or screen.
Your child can talk about simple problems with computer parts or programs using the right words.
Your child can explain how industrial growth, city life, new communication, and cultural changes affected life in the U.S. from the late 1800s to World War I.
Your child can choose and use different computer programs for different tasks, and understands that people like to use technology in different ways.
Your child can talk about how people's lives and jobs change when new computer technology is used.
Your child can explain the steps they took and choices they made when making a computer program, using the right words.
Your child understands how the War of 1812 and the Creek War affected Alabama socially, politically, and economically.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Technology — Computer Basics and Impacts
coherence_reason: Most standards are about basic computer skills and impacts, but two standards are unrelated social studies/history content.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will act kindly and responsibly when working with others online.
Your child earned credit in an elective class that encourages civic participation.
Your child uses correct capitalization, punctuation, and spelling when writing.
Your child works nicely and responsibly with others when using electronic communication.
Your child knows to give credit when using other people’s ideas or work in their programs.
Your child shows how to take care of electronic devices by handling them carefully, logging off properly, and keeping them away from food and water.
Your child learns to use technology and information responsibly.
Your child learns how to communicate and work together with others.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Technology and Digital Citizenship — Responsible Use and Collaboration
coherence_reason: Most standards address responsible use of technology and collaboration, but some focus on general writing conventions or civic participation, which are distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Responsible Technology Use and Digital Citizenship
General Writing Conventions and Civic Participation

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands what passwords are, why we use them, and can make strong passwords to keep devices and information safe.
Your child will keep their password secret and log off devices when finished.
Your child understands that rules help keep order, safety, and security at home, school, and in the community.
Your child understands why it’s important to keep login info private and how to log off devices properly.
Your child can explain why people use passwords.
Your child knows that a password helps keep information private and safe.
Your child understands that some information is private and should not be shared online.
Your child learns about security, privacy, sharing information, ownership, and copyright rules.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Digital Citizenship — Passwords, Privacy, and Security
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on passwords and privacy, but some also mention general rules and copyright which broadens the topic slightly.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will watch and describe what plants and animals (including people) need to live and grow.
Your child can use a model to show how different plants and animals, including people, need certain things and live in certain places.
Your child can explain with examples how plants and animals (including people) change their surroundings to meet their needs.
Your child can share ideas to help reduce how people harm the land, water, air, or living things nearby.
Your child can identify reasons why people moved to and settled in North America and what happened because of it.
Your child can tell the difference between needs (things you must have) and wants (things you would like) for families, schools, and communities.
Your child understands how American Indian cultures in North America were different based on where they lived, their resources, communities, economies, and beliefs.
Your child can find important physical features like ocean currents, winds, forests, rivers, and mountains that affected exploration and settlement in the Americas.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Living Things — Needs and Environments
coherence_reason: The cluster mixes standards about plant/animal needs and environmental impact with unrelated social studies content about settlement, needs vs. wants, and physical geography.
split_topics: Plant and animal needs and environmental impact
Human settlement, needs vs. wants, and physical geography

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will plan and do an experiment to see how different pushes and pulls affect how an object moves.
Your child can look at data to decide if a design works to change how fast or which way an object moves when pushed or pulled.
Energy
Your child explores how things move or stay still because of pushes and pulls.
Your child understands that air pressure from Earth’s atmosphere gets weaker as you go higher and pushes equally in all directions at any spot.
Your child knows that things fall down to the ground.
Track objects in motion.
Your child watches how objects move when pushed or pulled.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Motion and Forces — Pushes, Pulls, and Object Movement
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on pushes, pulls, and how objects move, but some reference gravity, air pressure, and energy as distinct subtopics.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can look at test results from two things made to solve the same problem and talk about what each does well and not so well.
Your child can give and accept helpful feedback when working on a project with others.
Your child can suggest a way to solve a problem by looking at information and thinking carefully, both alone and with others.
Developing Possible Solutions
Your child can work on improving a solution by testing and making it better.
Your child shows that there are many ways to solve a design problem and none are perfect.
Not applicable at this level.
Your child talks about how different designs have different features.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Engineering Design — Evaluating and Improving Solutions
coherence_reason: All standards focus on evaluating, discussing, and improving design solutions to problems, including collaboration and considering multiple possible solutions.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can break a problem into smaller steps and write out clear instructions to solve it.
Your child can create simple computer programs that follow steps in order and repeat actions to share ideas or solve problems.
Your child can find and fix mistakes in simple computer instructions that repeat steps.
Your child can make a plan that shows what a computer program should do, what it is for, and what should happen when it runs.
Your child can make and follow step-by-step instructions to complete everyday tasks.
Your child can use words related to giving and following directions.
Your child can find and fix mistakes in simple programs that use steps and loops.
Your child can make a plan that explains the order of events, goals, and results for a program.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Computational Thinking — Sequencing and Debugging
coherence_reason: All standards center on computational thinking, especially sequencing, planning, and debugging, but some focus on general step-by-step instructions for tasks while others emphasize computer programming.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child understands that computers use numbers or symbols to store and change information.
Your child can collect information and show it in different ways, like pictures, charts, or graphs.
Your child can use calendars and timelines to learn about events from the past.
Your child records and organizes information using pictures, numbers, words, symbols, or simple graphs.
Your child can make daily schedules, calendars, and timelines to organize events.
Your child can put events in order using schedules, calendars, and timelines.
Your child can recognize important symbols, customs, famous people, and celebrations from our state and country.
Your child can collect information and show it in different types of pictures or charts.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Data and Information — Collecting, Organizing, and Representing Information
coherence_reason: Most standards are about collecting and representing information, but some focus on chronological organization (timelines, calendars) and one on cultural symbols, which is a distinct concept.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can use tools and materials to build something that helps keep an area cooler by blocking sunlight.
Your child can ask questions and gather information to understand a problem that people want to fix by making a new or better tool.
Your child can clearly define an engineering problem and set limits to focus on solving it.
Your child learns that anyone can create and make new things.
Your child uses skills needed to make things when designing.
Your child understands that anyone can create or make new things.
Your child can design new technologies that might make daily life better.
Troubleshooting
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Engineering Design — Problem Solving and Creating Solutions
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on the engineering design process and creation, but there is some variation between hands-on building, defining problems, and general statements about creativity.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can draw or build a simple model to show how the shape of something helps it do its job.
Your child makes and uses simple models or designs a basic version of a solution to a problem.
Your child can solve questions alone and with others using models.
Share objects with a partner.
Modeling and Simulations
Your child can explain how models like globes or maps show real things.
Your child can make and share a step-by-step plan using objects or materials.
Your child chooses a familiar topic and uses drawing, talking, or writing to share information about it.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Science and Engineering Practices — Modeling and Using Models
coherence_reason: Most standards focus on using and creating models to represent real things or solve problems, but a few also reference general sharing or communication which is related but less central.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child will watch and share what the weather is like in your area to notice patterns over time.
Your child can compare old and current maps of Alabama to learn about its weather, land, waterways, transportation, politics, economy, land use, and population.
Your child can name important events and celebrations that happen in your town and in Alabama.
Your child knows water vapor moves through the air, forms fog or clouds, and falls as rain, hail, sleet, or snow.
Weather and Climate
Your child watches and shares what local weather is like to notice patterns over time.
Your child can describe a sunny day by noticing a blue sky, sunshine, and no rain.
Your child can keep a weather chart for a month, noting if it rained, was windy, hot, or cold, and decide if they need a sweater or umbrella.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Weather — Observing and Describing Local Weather Patterns
coherence_reason: Most standards address observing and describing local weather patterns, but some include unrelated topics like maps, events, and celebrations.
split_topics: Maps, land use, and community events

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can look at charts or graphs, spot patterns, and use them to make guesses about what might happen next.
Your child can recognize maps, globes, and satellite pictures.
Your child can find and explain patterns in charts or graphs to make guesses about what will happen.
Your child can figure out the meaning of new or tricky words by using different strategies while reading or learning.
Your child can figure out the meaning of new or tricky words based on what they read or hear in kindergarten.
Data Analysis
Your child understands that watching carefully helps them learn new things.
Your child can find and describe patterns in nature or things people make, and use them to make guesses.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Data Analysis — Recognizing and Using Patterns
coherence_reason: Standards mix data analysis/pattern recognition skills with vocabulary acquisition strategies, which are distinct concepts.

subject: Science
sample_standards: Your child can explain what caused World War I, what happened because of it, and why the United States joined the war.
Your child can explain their opinion about a community issue and give a reason for it.
Your child learns about scientists and engineers like Isaac Newton, Mae Jemison, and Ynes Mexia and what they do.
Your child understands how American democracy began and its main ideas.
Your child explains how science or new inventions can help people.
Your child uses different ways to figure out the meaning of new or tricky words when reading or learning.
Your child uses correct grammar and language rules when speaking and writing, building on what they learned before.
Engineering Design
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Engineering — What Scientists and Engineers Do
coherence_reason: The standards mention unrelated topics such as World War I, democracy, grammar, and vocabulary, alongside science and engineering concepts.
split_topics: English Language Arts: Vocabulary and Grammar

subject: Science
sample_standards: Career Research
Your child can find out information about things and actions in nature by watching carefully.
Your child can work with a partner to gather information together.
Your child can plan and do an experiment to test how matter can be solid or liquid depending on temperature.
Your child asks questions and defines problems to explore in science.
Your child plans and carries out science tests and investigations.
Your child plans and does an investigation to solve a local environmental or community problem.
Your child plans and carries out a simple investigation to solve a local environmental problem.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Science: Scientific Inquiry — Planning and Conducting Investigations
coherence_reason: Most standards are about scientific investigations, but 'Career Research' is unrelated and there are both general investigation skills and specific content (states of matter, environmental problems).
split_topics: Specific Investigations (States of Matter, Environmental Problems)
